romania.title: Hackathonul „Soluții pentru digitalizarea sectorului public” și-a desemnat câștigătorii
romania.summary: Hackathonul „Soluții pentru digitalizarea sectorului public”, organizat de The European Digital Innovation Hub in Transilvania (TEDIHT), s-a încheiat astăzi, odată cu desfășurarea Demo Day, marcând finalul unui parcurs de aproape… Articolul Hackathonul „Soluții pentru digitalizarea sectorului public” și-a desemnat câștigătorii apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Ministerul Educaţiei anunţă un nou set de 320 programe şcolare pentru învăţământul de liceu
romania.summary: Un nou set de 320 de programe şcolare pentru învăţământul liceal, elaborate în cadrul procesului de reformă curriculară, au fost aprobate, a anunţat joi Ministerul Educaţiei.

romania.title: MINA lansează din 9 mai 2026 două noi experiențe imersive: „Zeii Olimpului” și „În Inima Oceanului”
romania.summary: Două producții noi care aduc în spațiul imersiv mitologia greacă și lumea subacvatică, într-un format creat pentru copii și părinți deopotrivă. Din 9 mai 2026, MINA, Museum of Immersive New… Articolul MINA lansează din 9 mai 2026 două noi experiențe imersive: „Zeii Olimpului” și „În Inima Oceanului” apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: O ginecoloagă din Iași își îndeamnă pacientele, pe Facebook, să nu conceapă copii în post: „Sarcini oprite. Sau, după ce se naște, copilul moare în pătuț” / „Nu văd care este problema în a da un sfat din suflet”
romania.summary: Cristina Buzgar, medic ginecolog la Iași, afirmă, într-un mesaj video postat online, că „a observat, statistic vorbind”, că, în cazul copiilor concepuți în perioada posturilor religioase, există „mai multe sarcini oprite din evoluție, sau care, pe parcurs, dezvoltă anomalii, boli. Sau, după ce se naște copilul, pățește ceva suspect – moare în pătuț acasă, capătă …

romania.title: Centura verde din Videle, Teleorman, a fost finalizată prin plantarea a încă 2.000 de puieți. În ultimii trei ani, HP România a contribuit la acest proiect
romania.summary: HP Inc. România, alături de partenerii săi din programul HP Amplify și în colaborare cu Asociația MaiMultVerde, a marcat o etapă esențială în angajamentul său pentru sustenabilitate, printr-o amplă acțiune… Articolul Centura verde din Videle, Teleorman, a fost finalizată prin plantarea a încă 2.000 de puieți. În ultimii trei ani, HP România a contribuit la acest proiect apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.summary: București, 30 aprilie 2026. Programul educațional pentru liceeni Young Entrepreneur Generation (YEnG), fondat de Oliver Lepădatu, a fost selectat și calificat ca proiect promovat de Romanian Business Leaders (RBL) în… Articolul Programul de educație antreprenorială Young Entrepreneur Generation (YEnG), fondat de Oliver Lepădatu, devine proiect promovat de Romanian Business Leaders apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

George Soros – care oficial are 174 de ani, dar arată de cel mult 95, grație tratamentului cu sânge […] The post Soros riscă să-și piardă nemurirea din cauza natalității scăzute: „Găsesc tot mai greu sânge de copil” appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.summary: Chiar dacă plouă afară, asta nu înseamnă că tradiția micilor de 1 Mai trebuie să fie abandonată. ȘI mai ales suveraniștii români insistă că e nevoie de o mobilizare suplimentară pentru păstrarea acestei tradiții autentic românești. „Nu ești român adevărat dacă nu mănânci mici de 1 Mai”, a proclamat Giani-Ilarie Lemandroi, un suveranist din Berceni. romania.summary: Lansat în 2006 ca o satiră SF a unei lumi posibile, dar improbabile, filmul Idiocracy a ajuns să fie perceput azi cu totul altfel. Oamenii efectiv au devenit uluitor de proști, așa că pentru cineva din 2026 Idiocracy nu mai are nimic SF în el: totul în film pare banal, e ca și cum te-ai […] The post Oamenii au devenit atât de proști încât Idiocracy a fost mutat de la SF la documentar appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

romania.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
romania.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.title: Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate
romania.summary: De ce vorbim despre bullying Bullyingul nu mai este de mult o problemă „minoră” sau o etapă inevitabilă a copilăriei.... Articolul Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.summary: Descrierea postului: Părintele social crează un mediu de viaţă, îngrijire şi educaţie, caracterizat prin afecţiune şi stabilitate şi adecvat dezvoltării personalităţii fiecărui copil (în plan fizic, psihic, emoţional, social şi... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

medical.title: Communication from the CDC fuels skepticism about vaccines and science, research suggests
medical.summary: The scientific consensus is that vaccinations are neither causally nor statistically linked to autism. The US health authority CDC changed its official communication on this matter and instead emphasized a connection could not be scientifically ruled out.

medical.title: AI surpasses physicians on clinical reasoning tasks, raising the bar for more serious testing
medical.summary: In one of the largest studies to compare artificial intelligence and physicians on a wide array of clinical reasoning tasks including real emergency department data, a team of physicians and computer scientists at Harvard Medical School and Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center evaluated whether an AI system could do what physicians do every day: review a messy patient chart and use that information to determine diagnosis and next steps.

medical.title: Rare myocarditis after mRNA vaccination: Mitochondrial stress identified as a key factor
medical.summary: Myocarditis is recognized as a rare but serious adverse reaction to messenger RNA (mRNA) vaccination against COVID-19. Researchers at University of Tsukuba have demonstrated that mRNA vaccination induces the generation of mitochondrial reactive oxygen species under conditions of mitochondrial vulnerability, which triggers inflammatory cell death in cardiomyocytes, ultimately leading to impaired cardiac function.

medical.title: Q&A: Why feeling sick may be important for surviving infection
medical.summary: Symptoms such as fatigue, loss of appetite, altered sleep, and social withdrawal are often treated as inconvenient side effects of infection. While some scientists have suggested that they may serve a protective function, it is widely assumed that they're byproducts of being sick.

medical.title: Why squishy toys feel so good: What the NeeDoh craze reveals about brain and sensory needs
medical.summary: NeeDoh is the latest squishy sensory toy to go viral. Social media is reporting how these blobs of gel are flying off the shelves, and are in short supply.

medical.title: Genetic discovery may explain why pancreatic cancer is so difficult to treat
medical.summary: Pancreatic cancer can remain quiet for years, developing undetected before causing symptoms that lead to a diagnosis. Even after a surgeon removes a pancreas tumor, other cells often hide and erupt later. But University of Rochester Medicine researchers made an important laboratory discovery about why and how this happens—with the goal of targeting pancreas cancer with newer immunotherapy drugs.

medical.title: Smarter lighting could cut home energy use by 15% and improve health
medical.summary: Households could cut lighting energy use by more than 15% without sacrificing comfort, according to new research from the University of East London (UEL), which shows how improved lighting design combined with modern LED technology can make homes cheaper to run and better to live in. The paper is published in the journal Buildings.

medical.title: ALS may stem partly from failed protein cleanup in motor neurons
medical.summary: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a neurodegenerative disease that causes the progressive loss of motor neurons, which in most cases leads to respiratory failure within three to five years after diagnosis. In this context, a team from the Institute for Neurosciences (IN), a joint center of the Miguel Hernández University (UMH) and the Spanish National Research Council (CSIC), has identified that a cellular "selective cleaning system" for proteins: chaperone-mediated autophagy, is significantly reduced in patients, making it a potential therapeutic target to slow disease progression.

medical.title: Brazilian tree compounds block SARS-CoV-2 at multiple stages, lab tests show
medical.summary: A study has revealed that galloylquinic acids extracted from the leaves of Copaifera lucens Dwyer, a tree endemic to Brazil primarily found in the Atlantic Forest, have a multi-targeted effect against SARS-CoV-2, the virus that causes COVID-19. The work is published in the journal Scientific Reports.

medical.title: A five‑day course of magnetic brain stimulation could help autistic children communicate better
medical.summary: For children with autism spectrum disorder and with an intellectual disability, the options for improving communication and social skills are limited.

medical.title: Harvard scientists link gut bacteria to depression through hidden inflammation trigger
medical.summary: A gut bacterium may be quietly fueling depression through an unexpected chemical twist. Researchers found that when Morganella morganii interacts with a common pollutant, it produces a molecule that triggers inflammation—something strongly linked to depression. This finding helps explain how gut microbes can influence brain health at a molecular level. It also raises the possibility of new treatments that target the immune system rather than just the brain.

science.title: Early institutional care lowers life expectancy, study suggests
science.summary: Infants who grew up in early child care institutions in the 1950s have a much shorter life expectancy than the general population, according to a long-term study of individuals in Zurich. For the first time, the study provides robust evidence that early deprivation of affection and stimulation has serious consequences across the life span.

science.title: Kangaroos chart 'upside-down' evolution
science.summary: New research led by Flinders University argues thick tooth enamel helped kangaroos chart an unconventional evolution story, compared to the animals of other continents. A 50-million-year natural "experiment" among Australia's marsupials suggests that the outcomes of evolution are far from certain.

science.title: Hidden 3D atomic structure of relaxor ferroelectrics revealed for first time
science.summary: Materials called relaxor ferroelectrics have been used for decades in technologies like ultrasounds, microphones, and sonar systems. Their unique properties come from their atomic structure, but that structure has stubbornly eluded direct measurement.

science.title: Quantum computing's next dark horse emerges from a frozen surface, where almost nothing behaves as expected
science.summary: Quantum bits (qubits) are the fundamental building blocks of quantum information processing. A novel qubit platform invented at the U.S. Department of Energy's (DOE) Argonne National Laboratory exhibits noise levels thousands of times lower than those of most traditional qubits. "Noise" refers to disturbances in the environment that diminish a qubit's performance. The platform was built by trapping single electrons on the surface of frozen neon gas. The recent finding positions Argonne's platform as a strong contender in the field of high-performance quantum technologies.

science.title: 'More empowered': How online gaming benefits people with disability
science.summary: "You are more empowered because you get to be seen for who you are." These are the words of Link, an online gamer with disability—one of a group of 15 gamers with disability we interviewed as part of our new study, published in the Journal of Disability and Social Justice. (Names have been changed for privacy reasons.)

science.title: NASA Welcomes Morocco as 64th Artemis Accords Signatory
science.summary: The Kingdom of Morocco signed the Artemis Accords on April 29th during a ceremony in the country’s capital, Rabat, becoming the latest nation to commit to the responsible exploration of space. “It is my privilege to welcome the Kingdom of Morocco as the newest signatory to the Artemis Accords,” said NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman in […]

science.title: LHAASO discovers new extreme particle accelerator in the Milky Way
science.summary: The Large High Altitude Air Shower Observatory (LHAASO) has made a breakthrough in exploring the extreme universe. For the first time, the LHAASO collaboration has detected ultra-high-energy (UHE) gamma rays—with energies exceeding 100 trillion electron-volts (TeV)—from a gamma-ray binary system, LS I +61° 303. The discovery challenges existing theories of particle acceleration in extreme astrophysical environments.

science.title: Hold your nose and don't stop for a selfie: Why getting up close to a beached whale is a really bad idea
science.summary: The beaches of Sydney's Royal National Park have been disrupted by a pungent odor. And its source is drawing in more than just seagulls.

science.title: NASA Goddard’s Greenbelt Visitor Center Marks 50th Anniversary
science.summary: Trimmed in bicentennial pageantry, NASA opened a visitor center at its Goddard campus in Greenbelt, Maryland, in May 1976. Fifty years on, the Goddard Visitor Center continues to inspire through exhibits and programs on the past, present, and future of space exploration. When the visitor center first opened its doors (just a few weeks before […]

science.title: Massive reef expansion 20 million years ago may explain modern coral life's origins
science.summary: New research published in Science Advances reveals that the largest expansion of coral reefs in the past 100 million years happened about 20 to 10 million years ago, between Australia and Southeast Asia.

science.title: 'I don't believe no screens is possible': How parents manage devices and little kids
science.summary: Parents are told to avoid screens altogether in the first two years of a child's life.

science.title: NASA Explores Prioritizing First Response Drones in Crowded Skies
science.summary: Our streets are crowded with commuters and delivery vehicles, but when a police car or fire engine approaches with its lights and sirens on, drivers clear the way. In the coming years, drones for deliveries and other commercial tasks will become common in the skies over our communities, and NASA is working to ensure first […]

science.title: Rare Tyrian purple reveals elite Roman infant burials in York
science.summary: Two infants buried in Roman York were laid to rest in costly purple cloth normally reserved for emperors and members of the aristocracy, new research reveals. The babies were wrapped in a fine textile of Tyrian purple embellished with gold thread—a cloth of the highest possible status and luxury known in the Roman world.

science.title: Our study looked at teens' social media behavior—those from disadvantaged backgrounds face greater harms
science.summary: As social media becomes a central part of young people's lives, concerns are growing about its impact on their mental health. Yet public debates and measures tend to treat adolescents as one homogeneous group. We frequently ignore the fact that social media use does not affect all young people in the same way—nor does it have the same impacts on their well-being.

science.title: A lost galaxy called 'Loki' may be hiding inside the Milky Way
science.summary: The Milky Way galaxy grew into its current form with the help of smaller galaxies over time, which it has "consumed" or merged with. Astronomers are able to pick out which stars in the Milky Way came from other galaxies by identifying certain features, like the eccentricities of their galactic orbits and how many heavier elements they contain. Properties of some of the merged galaxies can then be determined when astronomers find collections of stars with similar features.

science.title: Medieval images may have triggered imagined soundscapes, reshaping how viewers experienced saints
science.summary: In cathedrals, such as Canterbury Cathedral in England, colorful stained glass and illustrated hagiographies tell the stories of saints through vivid visual narratives, inviting viewers into richly detailed scenes. Sometimes, this experience extends beyond sight, creating a deeper sense of immersion.

science.title: Hubble reveals spiral galaxy 53 million light-years away in striking detail
science.summary: In this new image by the NASA/ESA Hubble Space Telescope, a spiral galaxy glittering with star clusters is the center of attention. NGC 3137 is located 53 million light-years away in the constellation Antlia (The Air Pump). As a nearby spiral galaxy, this target offers astronomers an excellent opportunity to study the cycle of stellar birth and death, as well as giving researchers a glimpse of a galactic system similar to our own.

science.title: Scientists just discovered Africa is closer to breaking apart than we thought
science.summary: Beneath East Africa’s Turkana Rift, scientists have found the crust is thinning to a critical point, suggesting the continent is gradually breaking apart. This “necking” process marks an advanced stage of rifting that could eventually lead to a new ocean forming millions of years from now. Surprisingly, the same geological forces that are splitting the land may also explain why the region holds such a rich fossil record. Instead of being the birthplace of humanity, Turkana may just be where the story was best preserved.

science.title: This exotic particle could finally explain why matter has mass
science.summary: A major physics experiment has uncovered evidence for a strange new form of matter, where a fleeting particle gets trapped inside a nucleus. This exotic state may reveal how mass is generated, suggesting that particles can weigh less when surrounded by dense nuclear matter. The findings support long-standing theories about how the vacuum of space influences mass.

science.title: Gravitational waves may have created dark matter in the early universe
science.summary: In the chaotic first moments after the Big Bang, ripples in spacetime may have done more than just echo through the cosmos—they could have helped create dark matter itself. New research suggests that faint, ancient gravitational waves might have transformed into particles that eventually became the invisible substance shaping galaxies today.

science.title: Giant prehistoric insects didn’t need high oxygen after all, study finds
science.summary: Ancient Earth once buzzed with enormous dragonfly-like insects, and scientists long thought high oxygen levels made their size possible. A new study overturns that idea, revealing insect flight muscles weren’t constrained by oxygen after all. Their breathing system has plenty of room to expand, meaning oxygen alone can’t explain their giant forms. Now, researchers are searching for new answers—like predators or physical limits of their bodies.

science.title: Scientists just found what keeps plant cells from growing out of control
science.summary: Before seedlings can photosynthesize, they depend on fatty acids—and on peroxisomes to process them. Researchers discovered that the protein PEX11 not only helps these structures divide but also controls their size during early growth. When key genes were altered, peroxisomes grew abnormally large, suggesting internal vesicles normally keep them in balance. Remarkably, a yeast version of the protein fixed the problem, pointing to a deeply conserved mechanism across species.

science.title: NASA scientist says a mysterious "fifth force" may be hiding in our solar system
science.summary: Scientists are grappling with a cosmic mystery: why does the Universe behave differently on massive scales compared to our own solar system? While distant galaxies reveal clear signs of something bending the rules of gravity—often attributed to dark energy or a hidden “fifth force”—everything nearby seems to follow Einstein’s playbook perfectly.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.title: Hackathonul „Soluții pentru digitalizarea sectorului public” și-a desemnat câștigătorii
mix_items.summary: Hackathonul „Soluții pentru digitalizarea sectorului public”, organizat de The European Digital Innovation Hub in Transilvania (TEDIHT), s-a încheiat astăzi, odată cu desfășurarea Demo Day, marcând finalul unui parcurs de aproape… Articolul Hackathonul „Soluții pentru digitalizarea sectorului public” și-a desemnat câștigătorii apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Simpaticul George Soros, dar și alți miliardari ai planetei, se confruntă cu o criză fără precedent: copiii pur și simplu nu se mai nasc suficient de repede ca să le asigure lor rezerva de „elixir vital”. mix_items.title: Ministerul Educaţiei anunţă un nou set de 320 programe şcolare pentru învăţământul de liceu
mix_items.summary: Un nou set de 320 de programe şcolare pentru învăţământul liceal, elaborate în cadrul procesului de reformă curriculară, au fost aprobate, a anunţat joi Ministerul Educaţiei.

mix_items.summary: Două producții noi care aduc în spațiul imersiv mitologia greacă și lumea subacvatică, într-un format creat pentru copii și părinți deopotrivă. Din 9 mai 2026, MINA, Museum of Immersive New… Articolul MINA lansează din 9 mai 2026 două noi experiențe imersive: „Zeii Olimpului” și „În Inima Oceanului” apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

ȘI mai ales suveraniștii români insistă că e nevoie de o mobilizare suplimentară pentru păstrarea acestei tradiții autentic românești. „Nu ești român adevărat dacă nu mănânci mici de 1 Mai”, a proclamat Giani-Ilarie Lemandroi, un suveranist din Berceni. mix_items.title: Centura verde din Videle, Teleorman, a fost finalizată prin plantarea a încă 2.000 de puieți. În ultimii trei ani, HP România a contribuit la acest proiect
mix_items.summary: HP Inc. România, alături de partenerii săi din programul HP Amplify și în colaborare cu Asociația MaiMultVerde, a marcat o etapă esențială în angajamentul său pentru sustenabilitate, printr-o amplă acțiune… Articolul Centura verde din Videle, Teleorman, a fost finalizată prin plantarea a încă 2.000 de puieți. În ultimii trei ani, HP România a contribuit la acest proiect apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Programul de educație antreprenorială Young Entrepreneur Generation (YEnG), fondat de Oliver Lepădatu, devine proiect promovat de Romanian Business Leaders
mix_items.summary: București, 30 aprilie 2026. Programul educațional pentru liceeni Young Entrepreneur Generation (YEnG), fondat de Oliver Lepădatu, a fost selectat și calificat ca proiect promovat de Romanian Business Leaders (RBL) în… Articolul Programul de educație antreprenorială Young Entrepreneur Generation (YEnG), fondat de Oliver Lepădatu, devine proiect promovat de Romanian Business Leaders apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Oamenii au devenit atât de proști încât Idiocracy a fost mutat de la SF la documentar
mix_items.summary: Lansat în 2006 ca o satiră SF a unei lumi posibile, dar improbabile, filmul Idiocracy a ajuns să fie perceput azi cu totul altfel. Oamenii efectiv au devenit uluitor de proști, așa că pentru cineva din 2026 Idiocracy nu mai are nimic SF în el: totul în film pare banal, e ca și cum te-ai […] The post Oamenii au devenit atât de proști încât Idiocracy a fost mutat de la SF la documentar appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: O ginecoloagă din Iași își îndeamnă pacientele, pe Facebook, să nu conceapă copii în post: „Sarcini oprite. Sau, după ce se naște, copilul moare în pătuț” / „Nu văd care este problema în a da un sfat din suflet”
mix_items.summary: Cristina Buzgar, medic ginecolog la Iași, afirmă, într-un mesaj video postat online, că „a observat, statistic vorbind”, că, în cazul copiilor concepuți în perioada posturilor religioase, există „mai multe sarcini oprite din evoluție, sau care, pe parcurs, dezvoltă anomalii, boli. Sau, după ce se naște copilul, pățește ceva suspect – moare în pătuț acasă, capătă …

mix_items.title: SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025
mix_items.summary: Programul național Telefonul Vârstnicului – 0800 460 001 Sprijinul media a venit de la Spot TV – Pro TV, TVR, Antena 1, Antena 3 CNN, Antena Stars, Prima TV, Prima News, Pro Cinema, Pro Arena, Acasă, Acasă Gold, Trinitas TV, Speranța TV, Antena 3 Deva,... Post-ul SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025 apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București!
mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale
mix_items.summary: București, aprilie 2026 – Într-o lume în care cererea de hrană crește accelerat, iar presiunea asupra resurselor naturale este tot... Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase.
mix_items.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
mix_items.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Early institutional care lowers life expectancy, study suggests
mix_items.summary: Infants who grew up in early child care institutions in the 1950s have a much shorter life expectancy than the general population, according to a long-term study of individuals in Zurich. For the first time, the study provides robust evidence that early deprivation of affection and stimulation has serious consequences across the life span.

mix_items.title: Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate
mix_items.summary: De ce vorbim despre bullying Bullyingul nu mai este de mult o problemă „minoră” sau o etapă inevitabilă a copilăriei.... Articolul Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Părinte Social
mix_items.summary: Descrierea postului: Părintele social crează un mediu de viaţă, îngrijire şi educaţie, caracterizat prin afecţiune şi stabilitate şi adecvat dezvoltării personalităţii fiecărui copil (în plan fizic, psihic, emoţional, social şi... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Communication from the CDC fuels skepticism about vaccines and science, research suggests
mix_items.summary: The scientific consensus is that vaccinations are neither causally nor statistically linked to autism. The US health authority CDC changed its official communication on this matter and instead emphasized a connection could not be scientifically ruled out.

mix_items.title: Kangaroos chart 'upside-down' evolution
mix_items.summary: New research led by Flinders University argues thick tooth enamel helped kangaroos chart an unconventional evolution story, compared to the animals of other continents. A 50-million-year natural "experiment" among Australia's marsupials suggests that the outcomes of evolution are far from certain.

mix_items.title: Hidden 3D atomic structure of relaxor ferroelectrics revealed for first time
mix_items.summary: Materials called relaxor ferroelectrics have been used for decades in technologies like ultrasounds, microphones, and sonar systems. Their unique properties come from their atomic structure, but that structure has stubbornly eluded direct measurement.

mix_items.title: AI surpasses physicians on clinical reasoning tasks, raising the bar for more serious testing
mix_items.summary: In one of the largest studies to compare artificial intelligence and physicians on a wide array of clinical reasoning tasks including real emergency department data, a team of physicians and computer scientists at Harvard Medical School and Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center evaluated whether an AI system could do what physicians do every day: review a messy patient chart and use that information to determine diagnosis and next steps.

mix_items.title: Rare myocarditis after mRNA vaccination: Mitochondrial stress identified as a key factor
mix_items.summary: Myocarditis is recognized as a rare but serious adverse reaction to messenger RNA (mRNA) vaccination against COVID-19. Researchers at University of Tsukuba have demonstrated that mRNA vaccination induces the generation of mitochondrial reactive oxygen species under conditions of mitochondrial vulnerability, which triggers inflammatory cell death in cardiomyocytes, ultimately leading to impaired cardiac function.

mix_items.title: Q&A: Why feeling sick may be important for surviving infection
mix_items.summary: Symptoms such as fatigue, loss of appetite, altered sleep, and social withdrawal are often treated as inconvenient side effects of infection. While some scientists have suggested that they may serve a protective function, it is widely assumed that they're byproducts of being sick.

mix_items.title: Quantum computing's next dark horse emerges from a frozen surface, where almost nothing behaves as expected
mix_items.summary: Quantum bits (qubits) are the fundamental building blocks of quantum information processing. A novel qubit platform invented at the U.S. Department of Energy's (DOE) Argonne National Laboratory exhibits noise levels thousands of times lower than those of most traditional qubits. "Noise" refers to disturbances in the environment that diminish a qubit's performance. The platform was built by trapping single electrons on the surface of frozen neon gas. The recent finding positions Argonne's platform as a strong contender in the field of high-performance quantum technologies.

mix_items.title: 'More empowered': How online gaming benefits people with disability
mix_items.summary: "You are more empowered because you get to be seen for who you are." These are the words of Link, an online gamer with disability—one of a group of 15 gamers with disability we interviewed as part of our new study, published in the Journal of Disability and Social Justice. (Names have been changed for privacy reasons.)

mix_items.title: NASA Welcomes Morocco as 64th Artemis Accords Signatory
mix_items.summary: The Kingdom of Morocco signed the Artemis Accords on April 29th during a ceremony in the country’s capital, Rabat, becoming the latest nation to commit to the responsible exploration of space. “It is my privilege to welcome the Kingdom of Morocco as the newest signatory to the Artemis Accords,” said NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman in […]

mix_items.title: Why squishy toys feel so good: What the NeeDoh craze reveals about brain and sensory needs
mix_items.summary: NeeDoh is the latest squishy sensory toy to go viral. Social media is reporting how these blobs of gel are flying off the shelves, and are in short supply.

mix_items.title: LHAASO discovers new extreme particle accelerator in the Milky Way
mix_items.summary: The Large High Altitude Air Shower Observatory (LHAASO) has made a breakthrough in exploring the extreme universe. For the first time, the LHAASO collaboration has detected ultra-high-energy (UHE) gamma rays—with energies exceeding 100 trillion electron-volts (TeV)—from a gamma-ray binary system, LS I +61° 303. The discovery challenges existing theories of particle acceleration in extreme astrophysical environments.

mix_items.title: Hold your nose and don't stop for a selfie: Why getting up close to a beached whale is a really bad idea
mix_items.summary: The beaches of Sydney's Royal National Park have been disrupted by a pungent odor. And its source is drawing in more than just seagulls.

mix_items.title: NASA Goddard’s Greenbelt Visitor Center Marks 50th Anniversary
mix_items.summary: Trimmed in bicentennial pageantry, NASA opened a visitor center at its Goddard campus in Greenbelt, Maryland, in May 1976. Fifty years on, the Goddard Visitor Center continues to inspire through exhibits and programs on the past, present, and future of space exploration. When the visitor center first opened its doors (just a few weeks before […]

mix_items.title: Massive reef expansion 20 million years ago may explain modern coral life's origins
mix_items.summary: New research published in Science Advances reveals that the largest expansion of coral reefs in the past 100 million years happened about 20 to 10 million years ago, between Australia and Southeast Asia.

mix_items.title: Genetic discovery may explain why pancreatic cancer is so difficult to treat
mix_items.summary: Pancreatic cancer can remain quiet for years, developing undetected before causing symptoms that lead to a diagnosis. Even after a surgeon removes a pancreas tumor, other cells often hide and erupt later. But University of Rochester Medicine researchers made an important laboratory discovery about why and how this happens—with the goal of targeting pancreas cancer with newer immunotherapy drugs.

mix_items.title: 'I don't believe no screens is possible': How parents manage devices and little kids
mix_items.summary: Parents are told to avoid screens altogether in the first two years of a child's life.

mix_items.title: Smarter lighting could cut home energy use by 15% and improve health
mix_items.summary: Households could cut lighting energy use by more than 15% without sacrificing comfort, according to new research from the University of East London (UEL), which shows how improved lighting design combined with modern LED technology can make homes cheaper to run and better to live in. The paper is published in the journal Buildings.

mix_items.title: NASA Explores Prioritizing First Response Drones in Crowded Skies
mix_items.summary: Our streets are crowded with commuters and delivery vehicles, but when a police car or fire engine approaches with its lights and sirens on, drivers clear the way. In the coming years, drones for deliveries and other commercial tasks will become common in the skies over our communities, and NASA is working to ensure first […]

mix_items.title: Rare Tyrian purple reveals elite Roman infant burials in York
mix_items.summary: Two infants buried in Roman York were laid to rest in costly purple cloth normally reserved for emperors and members of the aristocracy, new research reveals. The babies were wrapped in a fine textile of Tyrian purple embellished with gold thread—a cloth of the highest possible status and luxury known in the Roman world.

mix_items.title: ALS may stem partly from failed protein cleanup in motor neurons
mix_items.summary: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a neurodegenerative disease that causes the progressive loss of motor neurons, which in most cases leads to respiratory failure within three to five years after diagnosis. In this context, a team from the Institute for Neurosciences (IN), a joint center of the Miguel Hernández University (UMH) and the Spanish National Research Council (CSIC), has identified that a cellular "selective cleaning system" for proteins: chaperone-mediated autophagy, is significantly reduced in patients, making it a potential therapeutic target to slow disease progression.

mix_items.title: Our study looked at teens' social media behavior—those from disadvantaged backgrounds face greater harms
mix_items.summary: As social media becomes a central part of young people's lives, concerns are growing about its impact on their mental health. Yet public debates and measures tend to treat adolescents as one homogeneous group. We frequently ignore the fact that social media use does not affect all young people in the same way—nor does it have the same impacts on their well-being.

mix_items.title: A lost galaxy called 'Loki' may be hiding inside the Milky Way
mix_items.summary: The Milky Way galaxy grew into its current form with the help of smaller galaxies over time, which it has "consumed" or merged with. Astronomers are able to pick out which stars in the Milky Way came from other galaxies by identifying certain features, like the eccentricities of their galactic orbits and how many heavier elements they contain. Properties of some of the merged galaxies can then be determined when astronomers find collections of stars with similar features.

mix_items.title: Medieval images may have triggered imagined soundscapes, reshaping how viewers experienced saints
mix_items.summary: In cathedrals, such as Canterbury Cathedral in England, colorful stained glass and illustrated hagiographies tell the stories of saints through vivid visual narratives, inviting viewers into richly detailed scenes. Sometimes, this experience extends beyond sight, creating a deeper sense of immersion.

mix_items.title: Brazilian tree compounds block SARS-CoV-2 at multiple stages, lab tests show
mix_items.summary: A study has revealed that galloylquinic acids extracted from the leaves of Copaifera lucens Dwyer, a tree endemic to Brazil primarily found in the Atlantic Forest, have a multi-targeted effect against SARS-CoV-2, the virus that causes COVID-19. The work is published in the journal Scientific Reports.

mix_items.title: A five‑day course of magnetic brain stimulation could help autistic children communicate better
mix_items.summary: For children with autism spectrum disorder and with an intellectual disability, the options for improving communication and social skills are limited.

mix_items.title: Hubble reveals spiral galaxy 53 million light-years away in striking detail
mix_items.summary: In this new image by the NASA/ESA Hubble Space Telescope, a spiral galaxy glittering with star clusters is the center of attention. NGC 3137 is located 53 million light-years away in the constellation Antlia (The Air Pump). As a nearby spiral galaxy, this target offers astronomers an excellent opportunity to study the cycle of stellar birth and death, as well as giving researchers a glimpse of a galactic system similar to our own.

mix_items.title: Scientists just discovered Africa is closer to breaking apart than we thought
mix_items.summary: Beneath East Africa’s Turkana Rift, scientists have found the crust is thinning to a critical point, suggesting the continent is gradually breaking apart. This “necking” process marks an advanced stage of rifting that could eventually lead to a new ocean forming millions of years from now. Surprisingly, the same geological forces that are splitting the land may also explain why the region holds such a rich fossil record. Instead of being the birthplace of humanity, Turkana may just be where the story was best preserved.

mix_items.title: Harvard scientists link gut bacteria to depression through hidden inflammation trigger
mix_items.summary: A gut bacterium may be quietly fueling depression through an unexpected chemical twist. Researchers found that when Morganella morganii interacts with a common pollutant, it produces a molecule that triggers inflammation—something strongly linked to depression. This finding helps explain how gut microbes can influence brain health at a molecular level. It also raises the possibility of new treatments that target the immune system rather than just the brain.

mix_items.title: This exotic particle could finally explain why matter has mass
mix_items.summary: A major physics experiment has uncovered evidence for a strange new form of matter, where a fleeting particle gets trapped inside a nucleus. This exotic state may reveal how mass is generated, suggesting that particles can weigh less when surrounded by dense nuclear matter. The findings support long-standing theories about how the vacuum of space influences mass.

mix_items.title: Gravitational waves may have created dark matter in the early universe
mix_items.summary: In the chaotic first moments after the Big Bang, ripples in spacetime may have done more than just echo through the cosmos—they could have helped create dark matter itself. New research suggests that faint, ancient gravitational waves might have transformed into particles that eventually became the invisible substance shaping galaxies today.

mix_items.title: Giant prehistoric insects didn’t need high oxygen after all, study finds
mix_items.summary: Ancient Earth once buzzed with enormous dragonfly-like insects, and scientists long thought high oxygen levels made their size possible. A new study overturns that idea, revealing insect flight muscles weren’t constrained by oxygen after all. Their breathing system has plenty of room to expand, meaning oxygen alone can’t explain their giant forms. Now, researchers are searching for new answers—like predators or physical limits of their bodies.

mix_items.title: Scientists just found what keeps plant cells from growing out of control
mix_items.summary: Before seedlings can photosynthesize, they depend on fatty acids—and on peroxisomes to process them. Researchers discovered that the protein PEX11 not only helps these structures divide but also controls their size during early growth. When key genes were altered, peroxisomes grew abnormally large, suggesting internal vesicles normally keep them in balance. Remarkably, a yeast version of the protein fixed the problem, pointing to a deeply conserved mechanism across species.

mix_items.title: NASA scientist says a mysterious "fifth force" may be hiding in our solar system
mix_items.summary: Scientists are grappling with a cosmic mystery: why does the Universe behave differently on massive scales compared to our own solar system? While distant galaxies reveal clear signs of something bending the rules of gravity—often attributed to dark energy or a hidden “fifth force”—everything nearby seems to follow Einstein’s playbook perfectly.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Am facut pasi spre succes. Majoritatea au fost spre bucatarie.

satire_ro.title: Soros riscă să-și piardă nemurirea din cauza natalității scăzute: „Găsesc tot mai greu sânge de copil”
